opening_line: Most AI engineer job posts are lying to you.
MLOps engineers make thirty percent more than ML engineers at the same company.
Hiring managers aren't just looking for Python skills anymore.
Your resume is upside down and it's costing you interviews.
Eighty-seven percent of hiring managers say they can't find candidates with AI automation skills — but here's what they're not telling you.
Every AI engineer knows Python and Docker, but the ones making 300K plus know this stack.
The highest-paying tech jobs in 2025 all require the same three skills that used to be completely separate.
Four skills that can get you to $180K within two years — but most engineers are learning the wrong ones.
If you've been wondering what an AI data engineer actually does all day, I'm about to break it down.
If you've ever wondered what a data engineer actually does all day, I'm about to break it down.
I focused on building the wrong thing for the first two years of my career.
Five years building systems in NYC taught me that learning as an engineer isn't about consuming more content.
Three skills are separating the candidates getting offers from the ones getting ghosted, and I'm about to show you exactly what they are.
Companies are hiring AI engineers who think in systems, not those who memorize ChatGPT prompts.
Tech recruiters just changed how they read resumes and ninety percent of engineers missed the memo.
The three most-shared pieces of tech job advice on LinkedIn right now — and why only one of them actually works.
Companies are hiring multi-skilled engineers at twenty five percent higher salaries than pure specialists — but most engineers are still building resumes the wrong way.
Security engineers who understand cloud architecture are getting hired at senior levels with three years of experience.
Three job titles are completely broken right now — AI engineer, ML engineer, and data scientist.
Sixty-two percent of tech job postings now mention AI skills even when they're not AI roles.
